comment.body: if the positioning is let's say, short speed in the front month and long speed in the second month, it can generate offsetting vanna exposures that require dynamic futures hedges when the spread moves... a single, aggregated vanna reading cannot capture these calendar-specific effects

comment.body:
Thanks for the reply! I can’t seem to let go of this vomma/volga topic.
After thinking it through for a while, I came to the conclusion that if dealers are currently short vanna, as seems to be the case through the end of the month, then they’re effectively short vega. That would imply they need to be long vomma/volga as a form of protection, to cap the risk of a volatility spike. So any vol spike would be capped (like you mentioned in your weekly post).
Does that conclusion make sense?

comment.body:
vanna is the spot vs vega, or delta vs vol.
so vanna doesn't imply short vega or long vega...
short vanna means shorter vega up, longer vega down,
long vanna implies, longer vega up, shorter vega down
comment.raw_body:
